images.caption:
Figure 2:Deception rates by language and model under simple hints(left)and complex hints(right).
Higher values indicate lower CoT monitorability because the model selects the hinted answer while hiding or misrepresenting the hint’s influence.
Deception remains high across languages, models, and resource tiers, with only modest reductions for GPT-OSS models under complex hints.

Figure 3:Distribution of hint-following behavior by model family.Left:When models adopt the hint (hint-used (HU)), they overwhelmingly do so via procedural reasoning (HU-Proc) rather than arithmetic manipulation (HU-Arith).Right:When models ignore the hint (hint-ignored (HI)), the distribution across arithmetic, factual, and random rationales varies substantially across families.

Figure 4:Token-level probability trajectories across generation for the GPT-OSS 120B model under different hinting conditions and final prediction outcomes. Each subplot shows the probability of generating tokens corresponding to options A, B, C, and D as the next token over normalized sequence positions, separated by high- (solid lines) and low-resource (dotted lines) languages. Plot I and II shows the simple and complex hint condition where the model ultimately predicts C. Plot III and IV presents the simple and complex hint condition where the model predicts the correct answer A. These trajectories illustrate how probability over candidate answers evolves during generation under different hint strengths and outcomes. Shaded regions indicate standard error and gray band (0.1-0.25) highlights the early generation phase. See AppendixA.1-A.3for more plots across layers and model sizes.

Figure 5:Deception rates under simple(left)and complex hints(right)across hinted targetsB,C, andD. High deception across targets shows that CoT monitorability failures are not driven by a single answer option.
Error bars show standard errors across 13 languages.

Figure 6:Deception rates across proprietary models across 13 languages under simple(left)and complex hints(right). High deception across models and languages shows thathint-inducedmonitorability failures go beyond open-weight models.

Figure 7:Answer-shift composition under complex adversarial hints by language resource level.
For each model, bars show the share of shifted answers landing on the cued optionCor miscued optionsBandD, separated by high- and low-resource languages.
Cued shifts generally decrease in low-resource settings, while miscued shifts increase, indicating more frequent erroneous hint application.

Figure 8:Token-level probability trajectories for the GPT-OSS 20B model at the last layer (layer 24) under different hinting conditions and final outcomes. Each subplot shows next-token probabilities for options A, B, C, and D over normalized sequence positions, separated by high- (solid) and low-resource (dotted) languages. Plot I shows the simple hint condition where the model ultimately predicts C; Plot II shows the same setup under complex hints. Plot III presents the simple hint condition where the model predicts the correct answer A, and Plot IV shows the corresponding complex hint condition. Shaded regions indicate±\pmstandard error, and the gray band (0.1–0.25) highlights the early generation phase.

Figure 9:Token-level probability distributions for the GPT-OSS 120B model across layers and hinting conditions. Rows correspond to different settings: (a) simple hint with final answer C, (b) complex hint with final answer C, (c) simple hint with final answer A, and (d) complex hint with final answer A. Columns represent layer depth, where I, II, III, and IV correspond to layers 1, 12, 24, and 36, respectively. Each panel illustrates the probability of generating tokens corresponding to options A, B, C, and D, highlighting how answer preferences evolve across both layers and hinting conditions.

Figure 10:Token-level probability distributions for the GPT-OSS 20B model across layers and hinting conditions. Rows correspond to different settings: (a) simple hint with final answer C, (b) complex hint with final answer C, (c) simple hint with final answer A, and (d) complex hint with final answer A. Columns represent layer depth, where I, II, III, and IV correspond to layers 1, 8, 16, and 24, respectively. Each panel illustrates the probability of generating tokens corresponding to options A, B, C, and D, highlighting how answer preferences evolve across both layers and hinting conditions.

Figure 11:Relationship between baseline error rate and deception rate for13 models and 13 languagesunder simple (∙\bullet) and complex (×\times) hints.
Under simple hints, most model–language pairs cluster near 100% deception regardless of baseline error rate.
Complex hints produce greater variation in deception, especially for the GPT-OSS models.

Figure 12:Option-letter bias check.Baseline error rates are shown for simple hints(left)and complex hints(right)when the hinted target is varied across optionsB,C, andD.
Simple hints induce high error rates across all hinted targets, while complex hints produce lower error rates for each target.
This pattern indicates that the effect is driven by adversarial hinting rather than a fixed bias toward optionC.
Error bars show standard deviation across 13 languages.

Figure 14:Relationship between baseline error rate and deception rate for closed-source models across 13 languages.
Each point represents a model–language pair under simple hints(∙)(\bullet)or complex hints(△)(\triangle).
Deception remains high across a wide range of baseline error rates, indicating persistent CoT monitorability failures under adversarial hints.
